acts.title: The bet on scale
acts.bandCopy: A generic network fed with GPUs and data crushes a decade of hand-engineered vision, and then depth itself gets fixed with two characters of algebra. The bet that opens the curve: general methods that scale beat clever methods that don't.
acts.gapCaptionAfter: 2016: attention already exists — invented in 2014 as a patch bolted onto recurrent networks. Nobody has yet tried keeping the patch and throwing away the machine.

acts.title: The architecture
acts.bandCopy: One equation replaces reading-in-order with looking-everywhere-at-once, and training goes parallel across the whole sequence. Parallel is what makes everything after this point on the curve physically affordable.

acts.title: The scaling era
acts.bandCopy: Next-token prediction turns out to contain every task, loss falls as a straight line you can budget against, and parameter count decouples from cost per token. Capability becomes something you purchase.

acts.title: Four threads at once
acts.bandCopy: Scale stops being the whole story. Four threads pull at once — seeing (CLIP), generating (Stable Diffusion), customizing (LoRA), and aligning (Chain-of-Thought, RLHF, Constitutional AI) — eighteen months that turn raw models into products.
acts.gapCaptionAfter: 2023–24: the frontier went closed. GPT-4 arrived as a technical report that named no parameter count, and the best ideas became trade secrets — and then, abruptly, they didn't.

acts.title: The reasoning era
acts.bandCopy: Pure reinforcement learning against an automatic answer-checker teaches a model to reason, and the weights ship openly, matching the best closed lab. The curve keeps descending — the open question is who draws the next segment.

papers.title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
papers.authorsShort: Krizhevsky, Sutskever & Hinton, University of Toronto
papers.hook: Two gaming GPUs beat a decade of hand-built vision — by ten points, in a contest where progress was measured in one.
papers.consequence: General methods that scale with compute and data beat expert hand-engineering — the bet every paper after this one repeats.
papers.cliffhanger: Everyone's answer was more layers. Then the deeper networks started losing to the shallow ones — not overfitting, failing to train at all.

rungs.text: Read the paper — or just the abstract and the results table; the 10.9-point gap is the whole argument.

Train an AlexNet-style CNN on CIFAR-10 in a free Colab — conv → pool → FC gets ~60% in minutes; add depth and augmentation and watch accuracy climb. The 'general methods + compute' lesson, felt firsthand.
Then do it the modern way: transfer-learn MobileNetV2 instead of training from scratch — the author's dogs-vs-cats repo is the AlexNet playbook, updated.

papers.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
papers.hook: A 56-layer network was losing to its own 20-layer version — until two characters of algebra, '+ x', made 152 layers trainable and swept ImageNet.
papers.consequence: That '+ x' skip connection now sits inside every Transformer block — the residual stream your interpretability tools talk about is literally this path.
papers.cliffhanger: Vision could now go as deep as it liked. Language was still trapped reading one word at a time — until a translation team asked what would survive if they deleted the recurrence entirely.

rungs.text: Read the paper — Figure 1's paradox (deeper plain nets have worse training error) and the one-line fix, y = F(x) + x, are the whole story.

Reproduce Figure 1 yourself: train plain-20 vs plain-56 vs ResNet-20/56 on CIFAR-10 and watch the deeper plain net lose until you add skip connections — the degradation problem and its fix on one chart you made.

papers.title: Attention Is All You Need
papers.hook: Eight GPUs, three and a half days, one equation — and the machine-translation record fell to an architecture that deleted the thing everyone thought language required: reading in order.
papers.consequence: Attention compares every token against every other, so cost grows with the square of context — this one equation is your API bill, your latency, and the reason prompt caching exists.
papers.cliffhanger: The recipe now existed. Nobody had asked what happens if you just... make it bigger.

rungs.text: Read the paper — the attention equation reads as a fuzzy database query, and everything else is engineering around it.

Build a GPT from scratch with nanoGPT's shakespeare_char config — you write the attention block yourself, and the paper's core diagram becomes ~30 lines of code. Karpathy's 'Let's build GPT' video is the guided path.
rungs.time: a weekend (a focused day with the video)

rungs.text: Go bigger: the author's tiny-llm-pipeline pretrains a ~12M-param GPT on TinyStories on a MacBook — and the author's taylor-transformer site walks the same architecture interactively.

papers.title: Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners
papers.hook: Trained on one dumb objective — predict the next word across 40GB of internet — it started translating, summarizing, and answering questions anyway. Nobody trained it to do any of that.
papers.consequence: The default question flips from 'how do I train a model for task X' to 'how do I elicit task X from a model that already learned it.'
papers.cliffhanger: Bigger kept working — but 'bigger helps' is a hunch, not a budget. Someone at OpenAI decided to measure exactly how much better, and found a straight line.

rungs.text: Read the paper — Figure 1's four rising lines (reading, translation, summarization, QA vs model size, with zero task training) are the thesis GPT-3 later proved at scale.
Reproduce the real GPT-2 124M — not a toy — with llm.c: ~90 minutes on an 8×A100 node at ~$14/hr, following Karpathy's own writeup.
rungs.time: 90 min compute + half a day setup

rungs.text: Budget version: fine-tune OpenAI's released GPT-2 weights on your own writing with nanoGPT — free, and the model starts sounding like you.
Or walk this paper's whole arc at toy scale — the author's tiny-llm-pipeline goes data curation → BPE tokenizer → pretrain → SFT/DPO → serving.

papers.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
papers.hook: Plot loss against compute on log axes and the points fall on a dead-straight line for eight orders of magnitude — capability suddenly had a price list you could read with a ruler.
papers.consequence: 'Wait for the next model' becomes a strategy with a price and a date attached — never build the thing one more order of magnitude erases.
papers.cliffhanger: OpenAI ran the ruler to its conclusion: 175 billion parameters, ten times anything before it. What came out the other side wasn't just a better text predictor.

rungs.text: Read the paper — Figure 1's three straight lines are the message; nobody needs to parse the exponents. Then note the 2022 Chinchilla correction: scale data with parameters.

Discover the law yourself: train three or four nanoGPT sizes (0.1M / 1M / 6M params) on the same data, plot final loss vs params on log-log axes, and watch the straight line appear — Kaplan's trends are visible at just 6.3M params, per an open replication with public training curves.

papers.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
papers.hook: At 175 billion parameters, you can teach it a new task by typing three examples into the prompt — no training run, no weights touched, nothing remembered when you leave.
papers.consequence: Few-shot examples are still the cheapest quality lever you own, and in-context learning is the mechanism that makes RAG work at all.
papers.cliffhanger: Dense scale had one brutal invoice: all 175 billion parameters fire on every single token. Google's next trick — let most of the model sit each token out.

rungs.text: Read the paper — or just Figure 1.2: accuracy vs number of in-context examples, per model size. You have personally lived this curve every time examples improved your prompt.

Plot the in-context-learning curve yourself: pick one task, run it at 0/1/5/10 shots against a strong API model and a small local base model, plot accuracy vs shots — ICL working at scale and barely existing below it, in one chart of your own.

papers.title: Switch Transformers: Scaling to Trillion Parameter Models with Simple and Efficient Sparsity
papers.hook: Google scaled a model toward a trillion parameters while compute per token stayed flat — a learned router sends each token to exactly one expert, and pretraining ran up to 7× faster on the same budget.
papers.consequence: Parameter count and inference cost are separate axes now — compare models by active parameters, not headline size, and you'll understand why API prices keep falling as capability rises.
papers.cliffhanger: While the text race compounded, a parallel bet at OpenAI came due: 400 million image–caption pairs, and a model that learned to see without a single hand-drawn label.

rungs.text: Read the paper — the routing diagram in Figure 2 is a load balancer you have already drawn for microservices, and the abstract's 7× is the payoff in one number.

Build a ~9M-param sparse MoE language model from scratch with makeMoE (the step-by-step Hugging Face blog walkthrough pairs with it): swap a dense FFN for 4–8 expert MLPs with noisy top-k gating, then log which tokens route where and watch specialization emerge.

papers.title: Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision
papers.hook: Type 'a photo of a dog' and classification becomes retrieval: trained on 400 million web captions, it matched a supervised model on ImageNet while using zero of its 1.28 million hand-labeled examples.
papers.consequence: 'Embed both sides into one space, then nearest-neighbor' is the workhorse under every vector DB, semantic search, and RAG retriever you ship.
papers.cliffhanger: Models this general made everyone want their own. But customizing GPT-3 meant a 350GB checkpoint per task — until someone noticed the changes fine-tuning makes are mostly redundant.

rungs.text: Read the paper — Figure 1's N×N matching grid (push the diagonal up, everything else down) is the whole idea in one glance.
Build a zero-shot classifier over your own photo library in ~10 lines with open_clip — search your photos by typing text. This is the vector-search intuition, live.
Train a mini-CLIP from scratch: ResNet + DistilBERT encoders, contrastive loss, Flickr8k's 8,000 image–caption pairs — and watch retrieval start working.

papers.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
papers.hook: Fine-tuning GPT-3 meant saving a 350GB checkpoint per task. LoRA matches it with a 35MB file — 10,000× fewer trainable parameters, 3× less GPU memory, zero added latency.
papers.consequence: Fine-tuning becomes a product feature instead of a capital expense — one frozen base model, a swappable 35MB adapter per customer.
papers.cliffhanger: Text had its cheap customization trick. Image generation was still hurling GPU time at 786,432 pixels per picture — until a Munich lab asked why the painting couldn't happen somewhere 48× smaller.

rungs.text: Read the paper — Figure 1 (big frozen W, skinny trainable bypass B·A) plus napkin arithmetic is the whole method: a d×k update costs d·k numbers; the rank-r version costs d·r + r·k.

Fine-tune a 1–3B model on your own MacBook with mlx_lm.lora and 200–500 examples — re-use the Taylor Swift lyrics from the author's taylor-lstm for the perfect callback: same data, LSTM in 2023, LoRA'd LLM now. Print trainable vs total params and see <1%.

papers.title: High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models
papers.authorsShort: Rombach et al., LMU Munich & Runway
papers.hook: A 512×512 image is 786,432 numbers; this model paints in a compressed space of 16,384 — and that ~48× discount on every one of ~50 denoising steps is what put image generation on gaming GPUs.
papers.consequence: The biggest cost wins come from changing the space you compute in — and shipping open weights against a better-funded closed rival turned its users into its R&D department.
papers.cliffhanger: Machines could now see, write, and paint. They still face-planted on grade-school word problems — and the fix required training nothing at all.

rungs.text: Read the paper — Figure 3's three zones (pixel space | latent space | conditioning) answer the real question: how does my prompt become an image? The cross-attention arrows are the Transformer page's equation, reused.
Train a DDPM from scratch on MNIST with the Hugging Face diffusion course — a ~2M-param U-Net, ~10 minutes on a small GPU — then sample it and watch pure noise denoise into digits you generated.
LoRA-fine-tune actual Stable Diffusion on ~20 of your own photos with diffusers' DreamBooth-LoRA scripts.

papers.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
papers.hook: Same model, same weights: show it worked examples with the steps written out and its math-word-problem score triples — 17.9% to 56.9%, from a prompt change you could type yourself.
papers.consequence: Answer quality becomes a runtime dial — more reasoning tokens, better answers, bigger bill; that's the extended-thinking toggle you flip today.
papers.cliffhanger: Prompting could unlock reasoning, but the raw model underneath still wasn't trying to help anyone. Turning a predictor into an assistant took three training steps and a team of about forty labelers.

rungs.text: Read the paper — the Roger-and-his-tennis-balls exemplar is the entire technique, verbatim, and the appendix carries full outputs for both prompting conditions.

Reproduce the headline with a cost meter attached: run 50 GSM8K problems in three conditions (direct / 'think step by step' / few-shot CoT), tracking accuracy AND tokens per condition — ~30 lines of Python, and 'buy quality with tokens' becomes a number instead of a slogan.

papers.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback
papers.hook: Human raters preferred a 1.3-billion-parameter model over the 175-billion-parameter GPT-3 — a hundred times smaller, and better, because three training steps taught it to actually try to help.
papers.consequence: The quality users feel is post-training, not scale — preference-tuning on your task can beat upgrading to a bigger model, and vendors' different 'personalities' are the reward model showing through.
papers.cliffhanger: It worked — powered by tens of thousands of human judgments nobody could read, audit, or argue with. Anthropic's counter-proposal: what if the values were just written down?

rungs.text: Read the paper — the three-box pipeline (SFT → reward model → PPO) is the most-redrawn diagram in the industry, and the 1.3B-beats-175B result is the abstract's first punch.

Turn a raw base model into an assistant with your own hands: SFT then DPO on Qwen2.5-0.5B with TRL and UltraFeedback preference pairs, on a free Colab T4 — then chat with the before/after checkpoints and feel the alignment layer.
Add PPO with TRL's PPOTrainer for the historically faithful version — or walk the author's tiny-llm-pipeline, which runs this same SFT→DPO alignment stage on a from-scratch tiny GPT.

papers.title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
papers.hook: Anthropic swapped tens of thousands of human harmlessness labels for 16 plain-English principles the model applies to itself — and got a model that was less harmful without refusing more.
papers.consequence: Supervising a model with a model plus written rules is now the standard pattern — LLM-as-judge, self-critique loops, and policy-in-the-prompt all descend from this paper.
papers.cliffhanger: AI could now supervise AI's behavior. The remaining question — could a model teach itself to reason? — sat unanswered while the frontier went closed. The answer, when it came, came with the weights attached.

rungs.text: Read the paper — the 16 principles in the appendix are literally readable sentences ('choose the response that is less harmful...'); pick one and argue with it.

Write your own 5-principle constitution and run the CAI loop by hand via API: the model answers a red-team prompt, critiques its own answer against your principles, revises — compare before and after.
Run the full open recipe: generate an AI-feedback preference dataset from a constitution and DPO-train Mistral-7B on it — Hugging Face's result resisted jailbreaks better without losing helpfulness.

papers.title: DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
papers.hook: Reward exactly one thing — 'the final answer is correct' — and competition-math scores climb from 15.6% to 71.0% with zero human reasoning examples. The polished model matched OpenAI's o1 and shipped its weights.
papers.consequence: Post-training is where differentiation lives now — if your domain has an automatic success check, it's a candidate for RL, and any closed capability gets replicated openly within months.
papers.cliffhanger: Frontier reasoning, reproduced in the open and distilled small enough to run anywhere, months after it was a trade secret. The moat is shallower than it looks — and every paper on this curve is a weekend build for $30 or less. The next node isn't a paper. It's you.

rungs.text: Read the paper — watch the AIME training curve climb from 15.6% to 71.0% with no supervised reasoning data, and read the model's own 'aha moment' transcript.

Run TinyZero: GRPO-style RL on a Qwen2.5-3B base model playing the Countdown arithmetic game, and watch the aha moment happen in your own logs — the model spontaneously starts self-verifying and revising. The ~$30 figure is the Berkeley authors' own claim; it needs two A100/H100-class GPUs, the one build of the fourteen that won't fit a free Colab. (Gentler on-ramps: philschmid's Mini-R1 walkthrough, Hugging Face open-r1's recipes.)
rungs.time: a weekend of wall-clock, realistically spread over a week
